title: Islamic World
subtitle: From Al-Andalus to Samarkand — geometry, calligraphy, tile, and dome.
intro: Islamic art spans three continents and fourteen centuries. Without figural imagery at its centre, it found other languages: endless pattern, abstract color, script as image, and vast cool interiors that turn geometry into prayer.

works.title: Dome of the Rock
works.meta: Umayyad · 691 CE · Jerusalem
works.desc: The earliest surviving major Islamic building. The gold dome marks the rock from which Muhammad is said to have ascended on the Night Journey.

works.title: Mezquita of Córdoba
works.meta: Umayyad of al-Andalus · 784 CE onward
works.desc: A forest of red-and-white striped arches from a great caliphate. A cathedral was dropped into the middle of it in the 16th century.

works.title: Hagia Sophia
works.meta: Byzantine & Ottoman · 537 CE onward · Istanbul
works.desc: A Byzantine cathedral that held the largest dome on earth for a thousand years, then an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, now a mosque again.

works.title: Alhambra
works.meta: Nasrid dynasty · 13th–14th c. · Granada, Spain
works.desc: A palace-citadel of courtyards, fountains, and muqarnas ceilings. The poems inscribed on the walls are part of the architecture.

works.title: Registan of Samarkand
works.meta: Timurid · 15th–17th c. · Uzbekistan
works.desc: Three madrasas framing a public square — Ulugh Beg's was home to one of the greatest astronomical observatories of the medieval world.

works.title: Topkapı Palace
works.meta: Ottoman · 15th–19th c. · Istanbul
works.desc: The sultans' residence for 400 years. Unlike European palaces, it's a scatter of pavilions in a walled garden — closer to a fortified village.

works.title: Süleymaniye Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · Sinan · 1550–1557 · Istanbul
works.desc: Mimar Sinan's masterpiece from the middle of his career — the architect's own tomb is in the garden outside.

works.title: Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed)
works.meta: Ottoman · 1609–1616 · Istanbul
works.desc: The Ottoman answer to Hagia Sophia across the square. More than 20,000 İznik tiles line the interior — the blue that gave it its Western name.

works.title: Shah Mosque, Isfahan
works.meta: Safavid Persia · 1611–1629 · Iran
works.desc: Seven-colour tilework on the scale of a whole square. Inside the dome, one clap echoes seven times.

works.title: Ardabil Carpet
works.meta: Safavid Persia · 1539–1540 · Iran
works.desc: Medallion carpet with 25 million knots. Made as a pair — the other half sold off piece by piece to repair this one. Now in the V&A.

works.title: İznik Pottery
works.meta: Ottoman · 15th–17th c. · Anatolia
works.desc: The workshop town that supplied tiles for mosques from Istanbul to Damascus. The signature tomato-red slip was a short-lived chemical triumph.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kairouan
works.meta: Aghlabid · 836 · Tunisia
works.desc: The oldest purpose-built mosque still standing. Its square minaret became the model for North African and Andalusi mosques for a thousand years.

works.title: Umayyad Mosque of Damascus
works.meta: Umayyad · 715 · Syria
works.desc: Built on a site already sacred to Jupiter, John the Baptist, and Mary. Byzantine mosaicists were brought in to lay the gold-ground interior.

works.title: Qutb Minar
works.meta: Delhi Sultanate · 1192–1220 · Delhi
works.desc: The tallest brick minaret in the world — 238 feet of red sandstone, carved with Qur'anic calligraphy as deeply as architecture.

works.title: Mosque of Ibn Tulun
works.meta: Tulunid · 879 · Cairo
works.desc: The oldest mosque in Cairo still in its original form. A vast brick courtyard under a spiral minaret — a shape borrowed from Samarra.

works.title: Po-i-Kalyan
works.meta: Timurid · 12th–16th c. · Bukhara
works.desc: A minaret, mosque, and madrasa across one square. Tradition says Genghis Khan spared the minaret because his hat fell off as he looked up at it.

works.title: Al-Azhar Mosque
works.meta: Fatimid · 972 · Cairo
works.desc: The mosque and university that became the intellectual center of the Sunni world. Continuously teaching since the tenth century.

works.title: Kutubiyya Mosque
works.meta: Almohad · 1150s · Marrakech
works.desc: A red sandstone minaret 250 feet tall — the template for the Giralda of Seville and the Hassan Tower of Rabat. Named for the booksellers who clustered at its base.

works.title: Jameh Mosque of Isfahan
works.meta: Seljuq onward · 8th c. – modern · Iran
works.desc: A thousand years of Iranian architecture layered in one building — two of the greatest domes in Islamic history stand a few meters apart.

works.title: Nasir al-Mulk Mosque
works.meta: Qajar · 1888 · Shiraz
works.desc: The "Pink Mosque" — stained-glass windows scatter colored light across Persian carpets every morning. Built late in the Islamic architectural tradition, as a kind of summary.

works.title: Gur-e Amir
works.meta: Timurid · 1403 · Samarkand
works.desc: The tomb of Timur under a ribbed turquoise dome. The grave was opened in 1941 with a curse; Germany invaded the USSR three days later.

works.title: Great Mosque of Samarra
works.meta: Abbasid · 851 · Iraq
works.desc: Once the largest mosque in the world. Its Malwiya minaret spirals upward 170 feet — a ramp you climb on the outside.

works.title: Mosque-Madrasa of Sultan Hasan
works.meta: Mamluk · 1356–1363 · Cairo
works.desc: A monumental stone box with a towering cruciform courtyard. One of the most audacious pieces of medieval Islamic architecture, by an unknown hand.

works.title: Shah-i-Zinda
works.meta: Timurid · 11th–19th c. · Samarkand
works.desc: A necropolis of tiled mausoleums along a single lane. The tomb complex for the cousin of the Prophet — and for every Timurid noble who wanted to lie near him.

works.title: Itchan Kala (Khiva)
works.meta: Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
works.desc: The old inner town of Khiva — mud walls enclosing minarets, mausoleums, and palaces so untouched it can still look like a stage set.

works.title: Selimiye Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · Sinan · 1575 · Edirne
works.desc: Sinan called it the masterpiece of his old age. Eight piers lift a dome wider than Hagia Sophia's — the height of Ottoman engineering.

works.title: Alcázar of Seville
works.meta: Almohad → Mudéjar → Christian · 10th–14th c.
works.desc: A royal palace begun by the Abbadids, expanded by Christian kings who kept the Islamic craftsmen — the result is Mudéjar: Moorish form under Castilian rule.

works.title: Giralda
works.meta: Almohad minaret → Christian bell tower · 1184–1198
works.desc: The Almohads built it as a minaret; after the Reconquista it became a cathedral bell tower. A ramp inside let the muezzin ride up on horseback.

works.title: Medina Azahara
works.meta: Caliphate of Córdoba · 936–976 · Spain
works.desc: The palace-city built outside Córdoba for Abd al-Rahman III — marble floors, mercury pools, gardens that moved. Destroyed in civil war within a century of completion.

works.title: Dome of Soltaniyeh
works.meta: Ilkhanid Mongol · 1302–1312 · Iran
works.desc: Second only to Hagia Sophia's in size when built. A blue-tiled double-shell dome soaring over the tomb of the Ilkhanid ruler Oljeitu — centuries ahead of its engineering.

works.title: Maqamat al-Hariri
works.meta: Abbasid · al-Wasiti · 1237 · Baghdad
works.desc: An illustrated manuscript of Hariri's picaresque stories — streets, markets, pilgrims, drunks, courts of law. The earliest surviving paintings of daily Baghdad life.

works.title: Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp
works.meta: Safavid · 16th c. · Iran
works.desc: A royal copy of Ferdowsi's epic with 258 miniatures painted by the court workshop — mountains with gold streams, demons in the rocks. One of the great illuminated manuscripts.

works.title: Bibi-Khanym Mosque
works.meta: Timurid · 1399–1404 · Samarkand
works.desc: Timur commissioned it after sacking Delhi — the largest mosque in the Islamic world at the time. The main dome collapsed within decades; centuries later the Soviets rebuilt it.

works.title: Great Mosque of Herat
works.meta: Ghurid onward · 12th c. · Afghanistan
works.desc: The Friday Mosque of Herat, its courtyard walls tiled in a dense cobalt and turquoise mosaic. Its tile workshops still operate beside the mosque, rebuilding what time erodes.

works.title: Khamsa of Nizami
works.meta: Persian · 12th c. poem, illuminated 14th c. onward
works.desc: Five epic romances — Khosrow and Shirin, Layla and Majnun, and others — illustrated by every Persian royal workshop for centuries. The British Library copy alone has 30 miniatures.

works.title: Pyxis of al-Mughira
works.meta: 968 CE · Carved ivory · Cour Visconti
works.desc: A small ivory box made in Córdoba for a teenage Umayyad prince. Every surface is carved with figures — musicians, lions, lovers, a man stealing eggs from an eagle's nest. The inscriptions include barely-veiled warnings about the instability of royal succession. Al-Mughira was executed by strangulation eight years later.

works.title: Baptistery of Saint Louis
works.meta: c. 1320–1340 · Hammered brass inlaid with silver and gold · Cour Visconti
works.desc: A Mamluk basin made in Egypt or Syria, covered in inlaid scenes of hunters, courtiers, and animals. At some point it entered the French royal treasury, and by tradition — probably invented — it was used for the baptism of royal children, including the future Louis XIII.

works.title: Naqsh-e Jahan Square
works.meta: Safavid · 1598 onward · Isfahan
works.desc: One of the largest squares in the world. Built by Shah Abbas to stage polo, military reviews, and the stateliness of a capital — framed on four sides by a mosque, a palace, a bazaar, and a private chapel.

works.title: Shah Cheragh
works.meta: Qajar · 14th c. onward · Shiraz
works.desc: A Shia shrine covered inside floor to ceiling in thousands of shards of mirror. Candlelight becomes a galaxy. Pilgrims hold a finger against the wall and watch their reflection splinter.

works.title: Al-Aqsa Mosque
works.meta: Umayyad onward · 705 CE · Jerusalem
works.desc: The congregational mosque on the Temple Mount, third holiest site in Islam. Rebuilt repeatedly after earthquakes; the wooden prayer hall facing the qibla dates partly to the 1930s.

works.title: Bou Inania Madrasa
works.meta: Marinid · 1350–1355 · Fez
works.desc: A teaching mosque of carved cedar, zellige tile, and stucco so fine it looks punched through paper. The Marinid sultan reportedly said the building cost was so beautiful it didn't matter what it cost.

works.title: Tomb of Jahangir
works.meta: Mughal · 1637 · Lahore
works.desc: Jahangir — Akbar's son, Shah Jahan's father — lies under white marble inlaid with pietra dura flowers, in a garden tomb of red sandstone. The minarets at the corners rise but never touch the roof.

works.title: Aljafería Palace
works.meta: Taifa · 11th c. · Zaragoza
works.desc: A fortified palace of the Banu Hud dynasty with a surviving Islamic core — interlaced polylobed arches, stucco arabesques. Now shares a building with the regional parliament.

works.title: Faisal Mosque
works.meta: Modern Islamic · 1986 · Islamabad
works.desc: A Turkish architect's vision of a Bedouin tent rendered in reinforced concrete and granite. One of the largest mosques in the world; a sharp-edged rejection of dome-and-minaret convention.

works.title: Hassan II Mosque
works.meta: Modern Moroccan · 1986–1993 · Casablanca
works.desc: Built out over the Atlantic on the king's sixtieth birthday. Its minaret is 210 meters — the second-tallest in the world. The hall's retractable roof opens to the sky.

works.title: Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
works.meta: Timurid · 1389–1405 · Turkistan, Kazakhstan
works.desc: Commissioned by Timur for a Sufi poet's tomb. Left unfinished when Timur died; the scaffolding on the portal front is six centuries old.

works.title: Mshatta Facade
works.meta: Umayyad · 743–744 · Jordan
works.desc: A desert palace facade dismantled and shipped to Berlin in 1903 as a diplomatic gift. Carved rosettes and winged lions in the softest yellow sandstone; too brittle to move today.

works.title: Qusayr 'Amra
works.meta: Umayyad · c. 723 · Jordan
works.desc: A desert bath complex covered inside with frescoes of wrestlers, bathing women, and the six kings of the world — Caesar, Negus, Khosrow — defeated or soon to be defeated by Islam.

works.title: Shalamar Gardens
works.meta: Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1641 · Lahore
works.desc: Three terraces of Persian-style paradise garden, 400 fountains fed by a canal that once ran from the Himalayas. Listed in danger after colonial-era road-widening destroyed the outer walls.

works.title: Tomb of I'timād-ud-Daulah
works.meta: Mughal · 1622–1628 · Agra
works.desc: "The Baby Taj" — the first Mughal tomb clad entirely in white marble and inlaid with pietra dura. Built by Nur Jahan for her father; the stylistic prototype for the Taj Mahal eight years later.

works.title: Agra Fort
works.meta: Mughal · 1565–1573 · Uttar Pradesh
works.desc: Red sandstone fort rebuilt by Akbar, later inner palaces added by Shah Jahan. Imprisoned here by his own son Aurangzeb, Shah Jahan could see the Taj Mahal across the river until his death.

works.title: Ali Qapu
works.meta: Safavid · early 17th c. · Isfahan
works.desc: Six-story royal pavilion overlooking Naqsh-e Jahan Square. Its upper music room has plaster cut into silhouettes of jars and vases — both ornament and acoustic resonator.

works.title: Chehel Sotoun
works.meta: Safavid · 1647 · Isfahan
works.desc: A pavilion of "forty columns" — twenty real wooden ones plus their reflections in the long rectangular pool. Interior halls are painted with the Shah's battles and banquets.

works.title: Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque
works.meta: Safavid · 1603–1619 · Isfahan
works.desc: The private royal chapel on the east side of Naqsh-e Jahan Square. No minarets, no courtyard — just a single domed hall whose tilework, buff and turquoise, changes color with the sun.

works.title: Koutoubia Mosque
works.meta: Almohad · 1195 · Marrakech
works.desc: A seventy-seven-meter minaret whose proportions became the model for Rabat's Hassan Tower and Seville's Giralda. Its red sandstone is visible from every part of old Marrakech; Almohad law forbade taller buildings within sight.

works.title: Great Mosque of Tlemcen
works.meta: Almoravid · 1136 · Algeria
works.desc: An Almoravid foundation whose carved plaster muqarnas dome and minbar count among the most elaborate surviving examples of North African Berber craftsmanship. Its minaret was added under the Zayyanids two centuries later.

works.title: Hassan Tower
works.meta: Almohad · 1195 · Rabat
works.desc: An unfinished minaret meant to top the largest mosque in the western Islamic world — the caliph Yaqub al-Mansur died and work halted at forty-four meters, half its intended height. Rows of stumpy columns still mark where the prayer hall would have stood.

works.title: Saadian Tombs
works.meta: Saadian · 1578 · Marrakech
works.desc: Lost behind a high wall after the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail sealed their entrance, then rediscovered from aerial photos in 1917. The Hall of Twelve Columns — gilded muqarnas vaults over sixty-six princely graves in white Carrara marble — is among the finest interiors in Morocco.

works.title: Mausoleum of Moulay Ismail
works.meta: Alaouite · late 17th century · Meknes
works.desc: Burial place of the sultan who built seven hundred palaces and commanded an army of black guards numbering 150,000. The sanctuary is one of the few in Morocco non-Muslims may enter; its painted cedar ceiling and tilework glitter around four clocks presented by Louis XIV.

works.title: Bahia Palace
works.meta: Late Alaouite · 1866–1900 · Marrakech
works.desc: A palace for the grand vizier Ahmed ben Moussa and his four wives, its 150 rooms arranged around courtyards of green tilework, painted cedar ceilings, and carved plaster. The name means 'the brilliant' — a name intended to impress visitors upon arrival.

works.title: Ben Youssef Madrasa
works.meta: Saadian · 1565 · Marrakech
works.desc: Once the largest Quranic college in North Africa, housing up to 900 students in tiny cells around a central courtyard. Cedar, marble, and zellij tilework interlock in concentric geometric schemes calibrated for the hours of daylight prayer.

works.title: Al-Attarine Madrasa
works.meta: Marinid · 1325 · Fez
works.desc: A tiny college next to the spice market whose four walls are totally covered in carved plaster, cedar, and zellij. Every square inch repays close looking: calligraphic friezes in Kufic and Thuluth, muqarnas capitals, and a delicately fountained courtyard only seven meters across.

works.title: Tiled Kiosk
works.meta: Ottoman · 1472 · Topkapı, Istanbul
works.desc: Mehmed II's pavilion inside the first courtyard of Topkapı, used for watching polo matches. Its façade of star-and-hexagon tiles in cuerda seca and its blue-and-white mosaic entrance are direct echoes of Persian and Central Asian craft recruited to the new Ottoman capital.

works.title: Rüstem Pasha Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · 1563 · Istanbul
works.desc: Sinan designed this compact mosque for the grand vizier Rüstem Pasha above a row of shops, hiding the prayer hall on a raised platform. It is clad inside and out in the most lavish İznik tiles Sinan ever deployed — flowers bloom on every surface.

works.title: Şehzade Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · 1548 · Istanbul
works.desc: Sinan's first imperial commission, built for Suleiman the Magnificent in memory of his eldest son Şehzade Mehmed. The architect later dismissed it as an apprentice work — in fact it established the centralized four-semi-dome plan he would perfect at Selimiye.

works.title: Eyüp Sultan Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · 1458 (rebuilt 1800) · Istanbul
works.desc: Built where the companion of Muhammad, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, was said to have died during the 7th-century Arab siege of Constantinople. Ottoman sultans were girded with the sword of Osman at his tomb; the courtyard is still a pilgrimage site across the Sunni world.

works.title: Badshahi Mosque
works.meta: Mughal · 1673 · Lahore
works.desc: Aurangzeb's great imperial mosque, faced with red sandstone and inlaid with white marble. Its courtyard holds 100,000 worshippers; for 313 years it was the largest mosque in the world.

works.title: Wazir Khan Mosque
works.meta: Mughal · 1641 · Lahore
works.desc: A small neighborhood mosque entirely encrusted in Persian kashikari tilework whose floral panels are unmatched in subcontinental Islamic art. Commissioned by Shah Jahan's governor of Punjab, it is the premier showcase of seventeenth-century Mughal ceramic craft.

works.title: Tomb of Bibi Jawindi
works.meta: Delhi Sultanate · 1493 · Uch Sharif, Pakistan
works.desc: An octagonal blue-tiled tomb whose eastern half collapsed in a nineteenth-century flood. What survives — Multani-style glazed brickwork in lapis, turquoise, and white on red — is among the finest examples of pre-Mughal funerary architecture in the subcontinent.

works.title: Shah Jahan Mosque, Thatta
works.meta: Mughal · 1647 · Sindh, Pakistan
works.desc: Shah Jahan built this mosque in thanks for the town's shelter after a revolt. Ninety-three domes and thirty-three arches are said to amplify the imam's voice so the entire congregation can hear without microphones — a feat of Mughal acoustical engineering.

works.title: Sidi Saiyyed Mosque Screens
works.meta: Gujarat Sultanate · 1573 · Ahmedabad
works.desc: Ten pierced-stone jali windows of the tree-of-life pattern — a tamarind tree winding through palm fronds — rank among the most reproduced motifs of Indo-Islamic art. The Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad adopted it as a logo.

works.title: Jameh Mosque of Yazd
works.meta: Azari style · 12th–15th c. · Iran
works.desc: A tall twin-minaret mosque faced in blue tile mosaic. Its high entrance iwan is featured on the Iranian 200-rial note.

works.title: Itchan Kala View
works.meta: Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
works.desc: The walled inner town of Khiva — minarets, mausoleums, madrasas all packed inside dun-colored mud-brick walls. So intact it can feel like a stage set.

works.title: Merv
works.meta: Seljuk · 11th–12th c. · Turkmenistan
works.desc: A caravan oasis on the Silk Road — Alexander's Margiana, the Sasanian second city, a Seljuk capital. The mausoleum of Sultan Sanjar still stands, its blue dome long since fallen in.

works.title: Imam Reza Shrine
works.meta: Safavid → Pahlavi · 9th c. onward · Mashhad
works.desc: The largest mosque in the world by floor area. Tomb of the eighth Shia Imam; seven courtyards; interiors covered in mirror mosaics that turn every surface into fractured gold.

works.title: Gumbad-e Qabus
works.meta: Ziyarid · 1006 · Iran
works.desc: A 53-meter brick tomb tower for Qabus ibn Wushmagir — a ten-pointed star plan tapering to a conical cap. No decoration beyond its geometry and its inscription band. The oldest surviving tower of its kind.

works.title: Piri Reis Map
works.meta: Ottoman · Piri Reis · 1513
works.desc: Surviving fragment of a world map drawn by the Ottoman admiral Piri Reis — the Atlantic coast of the New World only two decades after Columbus, drawn from prize charts captured in naval battle.

works.title: Ulugh Beg Observatory
works.meta: Timurid · 1420s · Samarkand
works.desc: A vast sextant sunk into the ground to measure stellar positions. The Zīj-i Sulṭānī compiled here tabulated over 1,000 stars — unsurpassed in accuracy until Tycho Brahe.

works.title: Reza Abbasi Paintings
works.meta: Safavid · c. 1600
works.desc: The defining painter of the late Safavid court — languid youths, calligraphic lines, single figures filling the page. He turned Persian miniature away from narrative tableaux toward portraiture.

works.title: Blue Qur'an
works.meta: Fatimid or Aghlabid · 9th–10th c. · North Africa
works.desc: Gold Kufic script on indigo-dyed parchment — no other known Qur'an was copied on so dark a ground. Pages scattered across the world's museums; its origin still argued about.

works.title: Khirbat al-Mafjar
works.meta: Umayyad · 8th c. · Palestine
works.desc: "Hisham's Palace" — an early Umayyad palace-bath complex with the single largest and most detailed mosaic floor of its era, a lion stalking gazelles beneath a tree of life.

works.title: Bab Zuwayla
works.meta: Fatimid · 11th c. · Cairo
works.desc: The southern gate of Fatimid Cairo — twin stone towers crowned by Mamluk-era minarets. A last public-execution site in the 19th century; heads on display above the gate into the 1800s.

works.title: Al-Hakim Mosque
works.meta: Fatimid · c. 1013 · Cairo
works.desc: A vast early mosque named for a Fatimid caliph who vanished in the night, never found. Its stone minarets with bulbous caps are strange — unlike any other in the Fatimid world.

works.title: Generalife Gardens
works.meta: Nasrid · 13th–14th c. · Granada
works.desc: The summer gardens of the Nasrid sultans — water channels running the length of the courtyard, fountains cooling long porticoes. Paradise as hydraulic engineering.

works.title: Qarawiyyin Mosque
works.meta: Idrisid · founded 859 · Fez
works.desc: Founded by a woman — Fatima al-Fihri — in 859. Its attached madrasa may be the oldest continuously operating institution of higher learning in the world.

works.title: Bab el-Mansour
works.meta: Alaouite · 1732 · Meknes
works.desc: An enormous ceremonial gate into the ruined imperial city of Moulay Ismail, flanked by columns from Volubilis — Roman marble recycled into Moroccan empire.

works.title: Chefchaouen
works.meta: Moroccan · 15th c. onward
works.desc: A mountain town in the Rif washed entirely in blue. Founded as a refuge for Moors and Jews expelled from Spain; the distinct blue wash is claimed variously as anti-mosquito, anti-evil-eye, or for keeping cool.

works.title: Al-Jazari Automata
works.meta: Artuqid · 1206 · Upper Mesopotamia
works.desc: An illustrated manuscript of ingenious devices — water clocks, a peacock hand-washer, a drinking elephant clock with automata on its howdah. Al-Jazari's engineering drawings are as clear as modern blueprints.

works.title: Qur'an of Ibn al-Bawwab
works.meta: Abbasid · 1000–1001 CE · Baghdad
works.desc: The earliest surviving Qur'an in cursive naskh script — the style that would outlast the angular Kufic. Copied by one of Islam's greatest calligraphers in a single year.

works.title: Mamluk Mosque Lamp
works.meta: Mamluk · 13th–14th c. · Egypt and Syria
works.desc: Blown glass enameled with gold, lines of Qur'anic inscription winding around the body. Lit, they cast filigreed shadows on the walls of vast Mamluk mosque interiors.

works.title: Sokollu Mehmed Pasha Mosque
works.meta: Ottoman · Sinan · 1571 · Istanbul
works.desc: One of Sinan's jewels — a small mosque on a sloping site, its mihrab wall studded with Iznik tiles and three authenticated stones from the Kaaba.

works.title: The Concourse of the Birds
works.meta: Safavid · Habiballah of Sava · c. 1600
works.desc: A single folio from the Mantiq al-Tayr — Attar's Sufi parable of birds that fly to seek their king and discover they are he. Painted by a master of the Isfahan school.

works.title: Damascus Room
works.meta: Ottoman Syria · 1707
works.desc: A complete wooden interior from an Ottoman-period Damascus merchant's house — painted panels, carved arches, a fountain floor. Dismantled and reinstalled at the Met; the everyday luxury of 18th-century Damascus.

works.title: Pierced Bowl by Hasan al-Qashani
works.meta: Seljuq Iran · late 11th–early 12th c.
works.desc: An openwork ceramic bowl of translucent white clay — molded so finely that liquid supposedly passed through but light did not. Signed by Hasan al-Qashani of Kashan.

works.title: Seljuq Standing Figure
works.meta: Seljuq Iran · 12th–13th c.
works.desc: A glazed stucco courtier in a jeweled headdress and beaded collar — from a palace ensemble that lined a reception hall. A rare survival of figural Islamic architectural sculpture.

works.title: Mamluk Minbar Doors
works.meta: Mamluk Egypt · c. 1325
works.desc: A pair of wooden doors inlaid with ivory and ebony in tight geometric stars — each star a polygonal meditation, complex enough to have inspired modern tessellation theory.

works.title: Mamluk Enameled Bottle
works.meta: Mamluk · late 13th c.
works.desc: Blown glass enameled in ruby, cobalt, and gold with Arabic inscriptions and arabesques — the same technique used for mosque lamps, scaled to a slender bottle.

works.title: Safavid Silk Animal Carpet
works.meta: Safavid Iran · 16th c.
works.desc: Silk pile with tigers, dragons, and simurghs in a flowering medallion field. Safavid court carpets were so coveted in Europe that Titian and Holbein painted them under Madonnas.

works.title: Kashan Silk Carpet
works.meta: Safavid · Kashan · 16th c.
works.desc: A smaller prayer-format silk carpet on a ground of deep red, with a directed arch and two columns — a portable mihrab, woven at unusually high knot counts.

works.title: Ottoman Christ-Medallion Lampas
works.meta: Ottoman Bursa · 16th–17th c.
works.desc: A silk-and-gilt lampas woven with roundels showing Christ in benediction — an Ottoman weaver's product for a Christian client within the Empire, presumably a liturgical vestment.

works.title: Lotto Carpet
works.meta: Ottoman · Ushak · 17th c.
works.desc: A wool pile carpet with a distinctive yellow arabesque on red — the "Lotto pattern," named for the paintings in which Lorenzo Lotto laid them under his Madonnas. Widely exported to Europe.

works.title: Ottoman Silk Kaftan Fragment
works.meta: Ottoman Istanbul · 16th c.
works.desc: A swatch of silver-brocaded silk in foliate medallions — from a ceremonial kaftan. Sultan's robes were preserved intact in Topkapı; fragments like this were cut from lesser garments.

works.title: Umayyad Clavi
works.meta: Umayyad / early Abbasid · 8th c. · Egypt
works.desc: A decorative tapestry-woven band ("clavus") from a Coptic-style tunic made under Muslim rule — late Roman textile traditions continuing seamlessly into the early Islamic period.

works.title: Iznik Tile with Saz Leaves
works.meta: Ottoman Iznik · c. 1550
works.desc: A stonepaste tile painted in the "saz" style — long feathery leaves curling against a white ground. Cut by the thousand to panel Süleyman's mosques; the signature of Ottoman decoration.

works.title: The Prince and the Petitioner
works.meta: Safavid · Vali Jan · late 16th c.
works.desc: A court drawing in ink and gold — a young prince seated on a rug, a humble man approaching with a petition. Vali Jan drew courtly manners with a faint ironic smile.

works.title: Portrait of Sultan Ahmet I
works.meta: Ottoman · early 17th c.
works.desc: A painted profile of Ahmet I, builder of the Blue Mosque — rendered in small miniature scale with the canonical turban and sable-lined robe. One of an album of sultan-portraits.

works.title: Kashan Luster Tile with a Couple
works.meta: Ilkhanid · Kashan · 1266
works.desc: A star-shaped luster tile with an embracing couple at its center — a scene from Khosrow and Shirin or a similar Persian love story. Once part of a tile facade on a Sufi tomb.

works.title: Bahram Gur Slays a Dragon
works.meta: Ilkhanid · 1330s · Tabriz
works.desc: A folio from the Great Mongol Shahnama — the hero-king drives a spear through a coiling dragon. Made for a Mongol court already patronizing Persian epic as their own.

works.title: Ilkhanid Bowl with Flying Birds
works.meta: Ilkhanid · Kashan · late 13th c.
works.desc: A stonepaste bowl with cobalt-painted birds in flight around the rim. Kashan was the Ilkhanid pottery capital — mass-produced luster wares for courtly consumers.

works.title: Timurid Royal Reception
works.meta: Timurid · Shiraz · 1444
works.desc: A double-folio frontispiece to a Shahnama — the royal court in a walled garden, a king receiving courtiers beneath a golden canopy. The Timurid workshops brought Persian painting to its quiet peak.

works.title: Alhambra Silk Curtain
works.meta: Nasrid · 14th c. · Granada
works.desc: A silk textile with Alhambra-style geometric and Kufic epigraphic panels — possibly hung in one of the palace's chambers. Granada under the last Muslim dynasty produced some of the finest Islamic textiles.

works.title: Fatimid Luster Bowl with Antelope
works.meta: Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat (Old Cairo)
works.desc: A ceramic bowl in metallic luster glaze — a single antelope at the center, stylized against a floral ground. Fatimid Egypt made luster ware a court art; the technique later migrated to Iran.

works.title: Fatimid Luster Bowl with Ibex
works.meta: Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat
works.desc: A companion to the antelope bowl — an ibex, horns curling over its back. The Fustat kilns flourished for two centuries before a fire destroyed the quarter in 1168.

works.title: Fatimid Bowl
works.meta: Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat
works.desc: A glazed ceramic bowl — plainer than the luster wares, but showing the wide painted-decoration tradition of Fatimid pottery. The base of the luxury market, not the top.

works.title: Iznik Tile Spandrel with Saz Sprays
works.meta: Ottoman Iznik · c. 1570 · fritware with underglaze
works.desc: A ceramic spandrel — the curving wedge above an arch — with cobalt tulips and saz leaves. Iznik in the 1570s was the workshop that tiled Süleymaniye in Istanbul and Selimiye in Edirne.

works.title: Iznik Dish with Artichokes
works.meta: Ottoman Iznik · c. 1540 · fritware
works.desc: A dish painted with heavy cobalt artichokes and vines — early Iznik still imitated the Ming blue-and-white that Ottoman merchants brought in from Chinese ports.

works.title: Qajar Wall Cover with Peacocks
works.meta: Qajar Iran · 19th c. · painted and printed silk
works.desc: A silk hanging hand-painted with peacocks and European-style portrait medallions — late Qajar taste blended Persian ornament with imported chromolithograph imagery.

works.title: Portrait of Raja Jagat Singh of Nurpur
works.meta: Mughal India · Bichitr · c. 1619
works.desc: A portrait by Bichitr of the Punjab Hills raja — a vassal ruler who would later rebel against Jahangir. Bichitr, signing his name in the lower margin, was Jahangir's most psychologically acute painter.

works.title: Rustam Slays the White Div
works.meta: Shah Tahmasp Shahnameh · Safavid Tabriz · 1530s
works.desc: A leaf from Shah Tahmasp's Shahnameh: Rustam reaching into the cave to split the White Demon with a dagger. This manuscript was the grandest Persian painting ever made; Tahmasp later gave it to Sultan Selim II.

works.title: Nushirwan Listens to the Owls
works.meta: Safavid Qazvin · 1550s · illustration to Nizami's Khamsa
works.desc: King Anushirvan halts his ride to hear two owls debate — the moral: a bad ruler leaves so many ruined villages that the owls get rich on the dowries of their daughters.

works.title: Khujasta and the Parrot
works.meta: Mughal India · Akbar's Tutinameh · c. 1560
works.desc: The merchant's wife Khujasta about to slip out for a night with a lover; the parrot begins the first of seven nights of stalling tales to keep her home — Akbar's early masterpiece of narrative painting.
